title: Supernovae That Leave No Black Holes
summary: Scientists are learning about black holes from space data. Black holes form when big stars explode in supernovae. Some stars are so huge that they explode in a special way called a pair-instability supernova. This explosion is so strong that it destroys the star completely, leaving no black hole behind. Researchers used data from detectors that sense gravitational waves, which are ripples in space from black holes crashing together. They found a gap in the sizes of black holes, meaning some sizes are missing. This gap suggests that pair-instability supernovae happen, stopping black holes from forming above a certain mass, around 45 times the Sun's mass. This helps us understand how stars die and what happens in the universe.

a missing range in black hole sizes
a way to measure mass using the Sun as a unit

background_read: Black holes are mysterious objects in space that come from stars exploding. When a star dies, it can become a black hole if it's big enough. Scientists use special tools to learn about them. Supernovae are huge explosions that happen when stars run out of fuel. Some supernovae are so powerful that they leave nothing behind, not even a black hole. This article talks about how scientists found evidence for these super-powerful explosions by looking at black hole sizes.
Article_Structure: WHO: Scientists and researchers studying black holes and supernovae.
WHAT: They found a mass gap in black holes, suggesting pair-instability supernovae destroy stars completely.
WHERE: In space, using data from gravitational wave detectors.
WHY: To understand how stars die and if some explosions leave no black holes.

perspectives.description: Researchers use data to learn about star explosions and black holes.
